text: Thank you for the opportunity to present our work.
I am Vahag Nikolian from Columbia University Medical Center.
We have no disclosures.
Today we will discuss a case of a low-lying Spagelian hernia and a robotic approach to repair.
Spagelian hernias occur from defects in the Spagelian fascia, which is composed of the aponeurotic layer of tissue between the rectus muscle medially and the semilunar line laterally.
The vast majority of these hernias will develop below the arcuate line.
Diagnosis can be difficult because these hernias may dissect between the oblique musculature, making external examination difficult to interpret.
Most hernias will be small and develop in older patient populations.
Diagnosis of a Spigalian hernia on CT scan is an indication for operative intervention as these hernias often have a narrow neck and are a threat to bowel viability.
Operations can be approached in a variety of ways, including open, laparoscopic, and robotic.
Today, we present a case of a seventy-one-year-old woman with a left Spagalian hernia with chronic incarceration.
She was previously worked up and offered an open repair and subsequently referred to us for a second opinion.
The CT scan reveals significant attenuation of the bilateral rectus abdomini and lateral musculature in the abdominal wall.
She has a notable diastasis recti.
The surgeon approaches the lower quadrant, revealing a defect consistent with a Spigellian hernia.
Given these findings, we offered her a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal repair with mesh.
During diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon visually identifies a low-lying hernia defect within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon positions three robotic trocars at the level of the umbilicus, similar to the setup for an inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon reduces the sigmoid colon from the hernia and identifies the Spigellian defect.
The surgeon develops a peritoneal flap, starting from the medial umbilical ligament and extending toward the anterior superior iliac spine.
The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks commonly seen during inguinal hernia repair, visible within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies a relatively large hernia sac and reduces it using a combination of blunt dissection and electrocautery, visibly manipulating the tissue to separate the sac from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies and transects the round ligament, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon works towards the femoral space, identifying herniated fat and reducing it from the area.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the aberrant obturator vessels overlying Cooper's ligament to avoid disruption during the procedure.
The surgeon reviews the anatomical landmarks after completing the dissection and evaluating all potential hernia spaces.

The surgeon identifies the external iliac vessels and nerves around the psoas muscle, examines the Spigelian defect, and assesses the potential space for an endurohernia, concluding with a view of the cleared femoral canal.

The surgeon introduces a large polypropylene mesh into the surgical field, securing it medially to Cooper's ligament and laterally to the transversalis fascia, ensuring coverage over the previously dissected areas.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running barbed monofilament suture, ensuring a secure closure of the abdominal cavity.
The surgical ports are removed from the patient's abdomen, followed by the extubation process.
She was discharged home the following day.
In follow-up, she was doing quite well and satisfied with her post-operative outcome.
This operation highlights the benefits of a minimally invasive approach to Spigelia hernias.
Diagnostic laparoscopy can aid in optimizing port positioning.
An open repair or laparoscopic intraperitoneal onlay mesh approach would be of limited use in this setting as the structure surrounding the defect would be at risk for injury.
A low-lying spagallion should be repaired with a preperitoneal approach to safely elucidate the anatomy and appropriately position the mesh with minimal fixation.
Thank you very much and I'd be happy to address questions.
